FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly uses ABCD Study data (Data Release 5.1), NIH-funded, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10; 'This retrospective cohort study uses data from the ABCD study, which is an ongoing and 10-year longitudinal study of neurocognitive development in adolescents.'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal fMRI cohort study analyzing ABCD data; linear mixed models examining cyberbullying victimization at T1 predicting brain activation, inhibitory control, and mental health at T2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Medical Internet Research 2026; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: The sole digital variable analyzed as IV is cyberbullying victimization (yes/no), which falls under the binding clarification 'cyberbullying alone fails IC2.' Social media use appears only as a covariate in all models (Table 1 lists 'Social media use, hours, mean (SD)' as a baseline characteristic; the statistical analyses section states it was 'included as covariates in all analyses'). No phone/SM-specific variable (time on social media, time on phone, named platform, texting, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient in any results table. Binding clarification: cyberbullying alone does not qualify under FT-IC2.

notes: Cyberbullying victimization is the primary IV; social media use included only as a covariate. Binding clarification in FT-IC2 explicitly states cyberbullying alone fails IC2. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Data Release 4.0; 'ongoing longitudinal study beginning in 2016 across 21 research sites in the US'; baseline mean age 9.5 years, N=8979.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Gaussian Graphical Models (GGM) and Graphical Vector Autoregressive Models (GVAR) on ABCD three-wave longitudinal data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology 2026; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Social media use (e.g., Instagram) and online chatting (texting + video chatting combined) are measured as separate screen activity variables with their own network edge weights paired with irritability (individual-trait mental health outcome). Temporal network results: 'TV-watching and social media predicted increased irritability over time (0.03 and 0.12, respectively), while online chat predicted decreased irritability over time (-0.03). Irritability predicted increased time spent on online chat (0.03).' Social media and online chatting are qualifying phone/SM-specific variables (named platform, texting, video chat); irritability is an individual-trait-level variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Network edge weights reported numerically: social media -> irritability edge weight 0.12; online chat -> irritability -0.03; irritability -> online chat 0.03 (temporal network). Cross-sectional network: irritability negatively associated with video streaming (edge weight -0.03 at Year 2). Supplementary temporal network with internalizing/externalizing: 'online chat and social media predicted increases in both psychopathologies (estimates ranging from 0.02 to 0.04).'

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential temporal network analysis (GVAR) with quantitative edge weight estimates reported for SM and online chat predicting irritability; supplementary GGM cross-sectional and GVAR analyses also reported.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply for two independent reasons: (1) The design includes both cross-sectional GGM analyses (at baseline, Year 1, Year 2) and longitudinal GVAR temporal networks, providing cross-sectional analyses with SM as DV — the cross-sectional carve-out applies. (2) The bidirectional/cross-lagged GVAR model has SM/screen time as both IV (predicting irritability) and DV (irritability predicting online chat), so SM is not DV-only. All three EC7 conditions are not simultaneously met.
notes: Social media and online chatting (texting+video chat composite) qualify as phone/SM variables under Step 1. Bidirectional design; cross-sectional networks also present. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Data Release 5.1; 'extensive longitudinal study has recruited over 11,500 children aged 9-11 years across 21 sites in the United States'; NIH-funded ABCD study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using linear mixed-effects models on ABCD baseline and 2-year follow-up data (n=7043).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescence 2026; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Social media ("how many hours do you: Visit social networking sites like Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, etc.") and social engagement (texting on a cell phone/tablet/computer + video chat combined) are measured as separate screen-time variables with their own regression coefficients paired with cognitive outcomes (individual-trait level). Table 3 (FCF outcomes): social media B=-0.50 (pFDR=0.006) for inhibitory control and attention, B=-0.99 (pFDR=0.039) for information processing speed, B=-1.40 (pFDR<0.001) for visuospatial sequencing and memory. Table 4 (CCF outcomes): social media B=-0.79 (pFDR<0.001) for verbal intellect, B=-0.28 (pFDR=0.019) for reading and language ability. Cognitive functioning (NIH Toolbox) is an individual-trait-level variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression beta coefficients reported in Tables 3 and 4 for social media, social engagement, passive watching, and gaming paired with five cognitive outcomes (inhibitory control, processing speed, visuospatial sequencing, verbal intellect, reading ability). All results include B, SE, t-value, p, and pFDR.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential linear mixed-effects regression analysis with quantitative B coefficients, SE, t-values, and FDR-corrected p-values reported for SM and individual-trait (cognitive) outcome associations in Tables 3-5.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply: the design uses screen time / SM as the IV (predictor) and cognitive function as the DV (outcome). SM is never the DV in any analysis — it is always the predictor. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis, which is not the case here.
notes: Social media and social engagement (texting+video chat) each have separate qualifying coefficients in Tables 3 and 4. Gaming is also analyzed separately. Screen time types are IV; cognitive scores are DV. INCLUDE.
